---
title: "HW 4.3 Reproducible Analysis"
author: "Mohammed Alghamdi"
date: "April 13, 2026"
format: pdf
---

# Busiest Airports Analysis

## Busiest Airports Table

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| label: tbl-airports
#| tbl-cap: "Passenger traffic for six major airports from 2020 to 2025."

# load required packages for scraping, cleaning, and table formatting
library(rvest)
library(dplyr)
library(stringr)
library(readr)
library(tidyr)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)

# Wikipedia page containing airport traffic tables
wiki_url <- "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_busiest_airports_by_passenger_traffic"

# extract all tables from the webpage
airport_tables <- read_html(wiki_url) %>%
  html_elements("table.wikitable") %>%
  html_table()

# assign years to each table (2025 to 2020)
years <- 2025:2020

# create empty list to store cleaned tables
cleaned_tables <- vector("list", 6)

# loop through first 6 tables and clean them
for (i in 1:6) {
  
  tbl <- airport_tables[[i]]
  
  # identify key columns
  airport_col <- names(tbl)[str_detect(names(tbl), "Airport")][1]
  code_col <- names(tbl)[str_detect(names(tbl), "Code")][1]
  passengers_col <- names(tbl)[
    str_detect(names(tbl), regex("passenger", ignore_case = TRUE))
  ][1]
  
  # clean and store data for each year
  cleaned_tables[[i]] <- data.frame(
    year = years[i],
    airport = str_squish(tbl[[airport_col]]),         # clean airport names
    iata = str_extract(tbl[[code_col]], "[A-Z]{3}"), # extract IATA codes
    passengers = parse_number(as.character(tbl[[passengers_col]])) # numeric passengers
  )
}

# combine all yearly tables into one dataset
all_airports <- bind_rows(cleaned_tables)

# filter for the required airports and organize the data
busiest_airports_tidy <- all_airports %>%
  filter(
    airport %in% c(
      "Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport",
      "Frankfurt Airport",
      "Beijing Daxing International Airport",
      "Dubai International Airport",
      "Tokyo Haneda Airport",
      "Los Angeles International Airport"
    )
  ) %>%
  # assign correct IATA codes
  mutate(
    iata = case_when(
      airport == "Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport" ~ "ATL",
      airport == "Frankfurt Airport" ~ "FRA",
      airport == "Beijing Daxing International Airport" ~ "PKX",
      airport == "Dubai International Airport" ~ "DXB",
      airport == "Tokyo Haneda Airport" ~ "HND",
      airport == "Los Angeles International Airport" ~ "LAX"
    )
  ) %>%
  select(year, airport, iata, passengers) %>%
  arrange(airport, year)

# reshape data to wide format for table display
busiest_airports_tidy %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = year,
    values_from = passengers
  ) %>%
  # format numbers with commas and keep missing values as NA
  mutate(
    across(
      `2020`:`2025`,
      ~ ifelse(is.na(.), "NA", formatC(as.numeric(.), format = "f", big.mark = ",", digits = 0))
    )
  ) %>%
  # order columns properly
  select(airport, iata, `2020`, `2021`, `2022`, `2023`, `2024`, `2025`) %>%
  arrange(airport) %>%
  # create final table
  kable(
    align = "lccccccc",
    col.names = c("Airport", "IATA", "2020", "2021", "2022", "2023", "2024", "2025")
  ) %>%
  # improve spacing and prevent cutoff
  kable_styling(full_width = FALSE, font_size = 10)

```

## Busiest Airports Plot

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| label: fig-airports
#| fig-cap: "Passenger traffic trends for six major airports from 2020 to 2025."

# load plotting package
library(ggplot2)

# create line plot showing passenger trends over time
busiest_airports_tidy %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = passengers, color = iata, group = iata)) +
  
  # add lines for each airport
  geom_line() +
  
  # add points at each year
  geom_point() +
  
  # label axes and legend
  labs(
    x = "Year",
    y = "Passengers",
    color = "Airport"
  )
```

Table @tbl-airports contains data on the number of passengers from six big airports in the years between 2020 and 2025. This table can be very helpful as it provides the actual figures for each airport. It also allows comparison for the same airport over several years. Graph @fig-airports facilitates the analysis of the trends by making them more visible. It emphasizes the change in the amount of passengers and their similarity/differences in recovery following low traffic periods.

```{r}
#| echo: false

# function to generate random points for Monte Carlo simulation
mc_points <- function(n, x_min, x_max, y_min, y_max){

  # generate random x values within range
  x_vals <- runif(n = n, min = x_min, max = x_max)
  
  # generate random y values within range
  y_vals <- runif(n = n, min = y_min, max = y_max)

  # combine into a data frame
  mc_data <- data.frame(
    x = x_vals,
    y = y_vals
  )

  # return the generated points
  return(mc_data)
}
```

# Monte Carlo Numerical Integration

## Small Multiple Visualization

::: {layout-ncol="2"}
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# generate Monte Carlo sample with n = 10
mc_data <- mc_points(10, -4, 4, 0, 0.4)

# compute function values and classify points
mc_result <- mc_data %>%
  mutate(
    f_x = dnorm(x),                           # normal density curve
    flag = ifelse(y <= f_x, "on/below", "above")  # check if point is under curve
  )

# estimate probability of points under curve
p_hat <- mean(mc_result$flag == "on/below")

# compute area estimate
estimate <- p_hat * ((4 - (-4)) * (0.4 - 0))

# create plot
ggplot(mc_result, aes(x, y, color = flag)) +
  geom_point() +                               # scatter points
  stat_function(fun = dnorm, color = "black", inherit.aes = FALSE) +  # true curve
  labs(title = paste("n = 10 | estimate =", round(estimate, 4)))
```

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# generate Monte Carlo sample with n = 100
mc_data <- mc_points(100, -4, 4, 0, 0.4)

# compute function values and classify points
mc_result <- mc_data %>%
  mutate(
    f_x = dnorm(x),                           # normal density curve
    flag = ifelse(y <= f_x, "on/below", "above")  # check if point is under curve
  )

# estimate probability of points under curve
p_hat <- mean(mc_result$flag == "on/below")

# compute area estimate
estimate <- p_hat * ((4 - (-4)) * (0.4 - 0))

# create plot
ggplot(mc_result, aes(x, y, color = flag)) +
  geom_point() +                               # scatter points
  stat_function(fun = dnorm, color = "black", inherit.aes = FALSE) +  # true curve
  labs(title = paste("n = 100 | estimate =", round(estimate, 4)))
```

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# generate Monte Carlo sample with n = 1000
mc_data <- mc_points(1000, -4, 4, 0, 0.4)

# compute function values and classify points
mc_result <- mc_data %>%
  mutate(
    f_x = dnorm(x),                           # normal density curve
    flag = ifelse(y <= f_x, "on/below", "above")  # check if point is under curve
  )

# estimate probability of points under curve
p_hat <- mean(mc_result$flag == "on/below")

# compute area estimate
estimate <- p_hat * ((4 - (-4)) * (0.4 - 0))

# create plot
ggplot(mc_result, aes(x, y, color = flag)) +
  geom_point() +                               # scatter points
  stat_function(fun = dnorm, color = "black", inherit.aes = FALSE) +  # true curve
  labs(title = paste("n = 1000 | estimate =", round(estimate, 4)))
```

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# generate Monte Carlo sample with n = 10000
mc_data <- mc_points(10000, -4, 4, 0, 0.4)

# compute function values and classify points
mc_result <- mc_data %>%
  mutate(
    f_x = dnorm(x),                           # normal density curve
    flag = ifelse(y <= f_x, "on/below", "above")  # check if point is under curve
  )

# estimate probability of points under curve
p_hat <- mean(mc_result$flag == "on/below")

# compute area estimate
estimate <- p_hat * ((4 - (-4)) * (0.4 - 0))

# create plot
ggplot(mc_result, aes(x, y, color = flag)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3) +                    # lighter points (many points)
  stat_function(fun = dnorm, color = "black", inherit.aes = FALSE) +  # true curve
  labs(title = paste("n = 10000 | estimate =", round(estimate, 4)))
```
:::

Monte Carlo integration estimates area using random sampling. As the number of points increases, the estimate becomes more stable and closer to the true value.

# Planning and Prompting GenAI Tools

## Data Wrangling Plan

The goal is to clean the data and make a clear graph.

Steps:

1\. Load the data into R.

2\. Look at the data to understand it.

3. Clean the column names.

4\. Check for missing values.

5\. Fix missing values.

6. Make sure columns have correct types.

7\. Create new columns if needed.

8\. Arrange or sort the data.

9\. Group the data.

10\. Summarize the data.

11\. Make the data tidy.

12\. Create a graph.

13\. Add labels and titles.

14\. Check the final result.

## Plan-Based Prompt Result

I used Anthropic Claude and gave it my detailed plan along with the dataset.

Claude cleaned the data, reshaped it into a tidy format, and created a clear visualization comparing the two groups over time.

The output included: - A cleaned dataset - A structured approach to tidying the data - A clear visualization of trends across time

The results showed that both groups remained stable at first and then decreased at the final measurement. The trends were easy to understand and clearly presented.

### GenAI Response (Claude)

Ran 3 commands, viewed a file, read a file

Here's a full walkthrough of every step:

Step 1 — Load: 16 rows × 8 columns, all numeric (float64). The first 4 columns were the Treatment group, the last 4 were Control.

Step 2 — Rename columns: Original names (y0, y1, y2, y3, y0.1…) were unclear, so they were renamed to treat_base, treat_y1, treat_y2, treat_y3, ctrl_base, ctrl_y1, ctrl_y2, ctrl_y3.

Step 3 — Missing values: Row 15 in the Treatment group had all 4 values missing (NaN). The Control group had no missing values.

Step 4 — Fix missing values: Filled the 4 missing Treatment values with their respective column means — a standard imputation approach that doesn't distort the averages.

Step 5 — Types: All columns were already float64 — no conversion needed.

Step 6–8 — Reshape & group: The wide table was melted into a tidy long format with three columns: Time, Calcium, and Group. This makes it easy to compute group means and plot.

Step 9 — Summarize (means):

Treatment: 72.4, 73.3, 72.5, 64.8\
Control: 69.3, 70.7, 71.2, 64.5

What the plot tells us in plain words:

For both groups, the initial calcium level was almost the same (\~69-72). There wasn't much change in calcium levels during Baseline up until Year 2 for both groups. However, there is a noticeable decline in calcium for both groups at Year 3, where the levels went down to about 64-65. The Treatment group had higher calcium levels compared to the Control, although the trends were almost the same for both.

There is overlap in the error bars, indicating that the differences between both groups were insignificant.

### Visualization from Claude

![](images/calcium_plot.png)

The graph below illustrates the changes in average levels of calcium over time between the treatment group and the control group. Both groups have almost equal levels initially, and the levels stay relatively constant until Year 2, after which there is a drop off in both cases. It can be seen that the treatment group is always just above the control group.

### Generic Prompt Result

In the graph presented below, one can find out about the trends of average calcium levels between two groups – the treatment group and the control group. They have quite close levels at the beginning, and they remain fairly constant until the Year 2, when both of them start to decline. However, it is possible to note that throughout the process the treatment group's level stays slightly higher than the control group's.

The generic prompt provided a similar kind of visualization; however, there was no particular step-by-step approach followed while explaining the results.

On the whole, the generic prompt worked but not as efficiently as the plan-based one.

### Comparison

The plan-driven prompt aided in generating an organized response. Claude was able to strictly adhere to the prompts by cleansing the data, managing the missing values, reconfiguring the dataset, and summarizing the outcomes. The output generated from the prompt was easily comprehensible.

The use of a plan-based prompt is better than that of a generic prompt since a plan gives guidance and structure on what to do next. While a generic response will still be able to give the desired result, it may lack accuracy or be unorganized.

In summary, utilizing plans helps generate better responses since they guide the GenAI tool through the process.

## Reflection

Throughout STAT 184, I've been introduced to various ways of working with data utilizing data verbs and viewing data as an object that can be processed at each step of the way. Rather than writing elaborate lines of code, I focused on learning to use simple and straightforward functions like filter(), select(), mutate(), and summarise() to execute certain operations on a dataset.

The notion of having cases represent each row in the table was one of the core themes that I have come across in STAT 184. To achieve this, it's possible to use pivot_wider() or other similar functions in R. Moreover, I have been introduced to such concepts as group_by() where data gets sorted, followed by summarise() and count().

Chaining commands became one of the techniques used in this course to create efficient code using the pipe operator (\|\>) which allows performing actions sequentially and, therefore, improves readability.

Furthermore, throughout the course, I have been practicing creating tables and other forms of outputting data. I have learned how to use the kable() function to generate tables that are visually appealing and easy for readers to perceive.

Lastly, another valuable concept learned during STAT 184 classes is the understanding of the principle of simulation based on the Monte Carlo method. By increasing the sample size used in the simulations, I managed to see more precise estimates being produced.

## GenAI Usage Appendix

Tool Used: Anthropic Claude\
Version: Latest available version (Claude)\
Date Used: April 15, 2026

Prompt Used: I uploaded the Calcium.csv dataset and asked Claude to follow my data wrangling plan step by step. The plan included cleaning the data, handling missing values, reshaping the dataset into a tidy format, summarizing the results, and creating a visualization comparing treatment and control groups over time.

Response: Ran 3 commands, viewed a file, read a file

### GenAI Response (Claude)

Ran 3 commands, viewed a file, read a file

Here's a full walkthrough of every step:

Step 1 — Load: 16 rows × 8 columns, all numeric (float64). The first 4 columns were the Treatment group, the last 4 were Control.

Step 2 — Rename columns: Original names (y0, y1, y2, y3, y0.1…) were unclear, so they were renamed to treat_base, treat_y1, treat_y2, treat_y3, ctrl_base, ctrl_y1, ctrl_y2, ctrl_y3.

Step 3 — Missing values: Row 15 in the Treatment group had all 4 values missing (NaN). The Control group had no missing values.

Step 4 — Fix missing values: Filled the 4 missing Treatment values with their respective column means — a standard imputation approach that doesn't distort the averages.

Step 5 — Types: All columns were already float64 — no conversion needed.

Step 6–8 — Reshape & group: The wide table was melted into a tidy long format with three columns: Time, Calcium, and Group. This makes it easy to compute group means and plot.

Step 9 — Summarize (means):

Treatment: 72.4, 73.3, 72.5, 64.8\
Control: 69.3, 70.7, 71.2, 64.5

What the plot tells us in plain words:

Both groups started with similar calcium levels at baseline (69–72).\
Calcium stayed relatively stable from Baseline through Year 2 in both groups.\
Then both groups show a notable drop at Year 3, falling to around 64–65.\
The Treatment group was consistently slightly higher than Control throughout, but the two groups follow nearly identical trends suggesting the treatment had minimal effect on the overall calcium trajectory.

The error bars overlap at all time points, meaning the difference between groups is likely not statistically significant.

## Code Appendix

All code used in this analysis is included in the Quarto document but hidden from the main report. The code was used to:

-   Load and clean the datasets\
-   Create summary tables\
-   Generate visualizations\
-   Perform Monte Carlo simulation

The code is properly formatted and commented within the Quarto file.
